Re
Fern Polrrrcnl PRACTrces CoMMrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 r Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
December 19,2007
Michael Gogna
Meyers Nave Riback Silver & Wilson
401 Mendocino Avenue, Suite 100
Santa Rosa, Califomia 95401
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.I-07-188
Dear Mr. Gogna
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Healdsburg City
Council Member Lisa Wittke Schaffner regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Please noG that because we lack siecific
information as to the effect each government decision may have upon a source of income
to Councilmember Schaffner, we are providing informal assistance. Informal assistance
does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(cX3).) Please also note that our
advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the
application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of
interest or Government Code Section 1090.
QUESTTONS
l. Do the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions bar Councilmember Schaffner
from participating in governmental decisions that will have a financial effect on business
entities that are members of the Sonoma County Alliance, a nonprofit entity which
Councilmember Schaffner serves as its executive director?
2. Does the "nexus test" disqualify Councilmember Schaffner from making,
participating in making, or influencing city council decisions that may affect the Sonoma
County Alliance?
' The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All
statutory ref'erences are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulationi of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through I tt997 of Title 2 of the Calitbrnia
Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the Califbrnia Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.
rfl2r


File No. I-07-188
Page No. 2
CONCLUSIONS
1. No. Councilmember Schaffner does not have an economic interest in the
business entities merely by virtue of their membership in the Sonoma County Alliance
provided, however, that either acting alone or in concert with other members, they do not
control the decisions of the Sonoma County Alliance.
2. If a city council decision regarding the Saggio Hills Project will have any
reasonably foreseeable financial effect on the Sonoma County Alliance, then the nexus
test would operate to disqualify Councilmember Schaffner from making, participating in
making, or influencing that decision.
FACTS
The Sonoma County Alliance (the "SCA") is a voluntary membership
organization composed of business, agricultural, labor, and individual interests focused
on education and advocacy concerning business and economic issues. It is organized as a
California nonprofit corporation under Section 501(cX7) of the Intemal Revenue Code.
The purpose of the SCA is to promote the business climate and economic wellbeing of
Sonoma County. In this regard, the SCA seeks to provide input to elected officials on
matters involving public policy, economic development, housing, taxation, and similar
matters. The SCA also encourages its members to participate in countywide legislative
and political affairs.
There are approximately 340 dues paying members of the SCA. The SCA has 5l
directors on its board of directors. Eighteen directors serve on an executive committee.
Three members of the executive committee (president, vice-president and treasurer) serve
as executive officers. Lisa Wittke Schaffner is the executive director and the sole
salaried employee of the SCA. She is also a city councilmember of the city of
Healdsburg. Her work is supervised by the executive officers who are also responsible
for negotiating the terms of her employment. Her job duties as executive director include
responsibility for administrative matters and development of written policy statements on
matters relating to the business environment within Sonoma County. These statements
only become the official policy of the SCA if approved by both its board of directors and
executive committee. The position of executive director carries no voting rights.
An application for development of a project called the "saggio Hills Project" is
currently pending in the City of Healdsburg. The developer is Sonoma Luxury Resorts,
LLC. Two members of Sonoma Luxury Resorts, LLC, Tony Korman Development and
Saggio Hills Development Company, are members of the SCA and each pays annual dues
of $350 to the SCA. Tony Korman is a principal in Tony Korman Development and
Saggio Hills Development Company. Mr. Korman serves on the executive committee of
the SCA. He is not an executive officer and he has never participated in any discussions
regarding Ms. Schaffner's employment contract. Neither Tony Korman Development


File No. I-07-188
Page No. 3
nor Saggio Hills Development Company, whether acting alone or in concert with other
members, controls the decisions of the SCA.
The development application for the Saggio Hills Project seeks the following
entitlements, which will come before Ms. Schaffner in her role as a city councilmember:
EIR certification, annexation, general plan amendment, adoption of specific plan, zoning
ordinance amendment, subdivision map approvals, and development agreement. City
council decisions regarding the application will have a financial effect on Tony Korman
Development and Saggio Hills Development Company.
AI\ALYSIS
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).)
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
The Commission has adopted an eighrstep standard analysis for deciding whether
an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b).) The general
rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a
governmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one
or more of his or her financial interests.
Step 1. Is Councilmember Schaffner a "public oflicial" within the meaning
of section 87100?
Section 82048 defines a public official as "every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local govemment agency." As a member of the city council of the
City of Healdsburg, which is a local government agency, Ms. Schaffner is a public
official. Therefore, she may not make, participate in making, or otherwise use her
position to influence any decisions that will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on any of her economic interests.
Step 2. Will Councilmember Schaffner be making, participating in making
or influencing a governmental decision?
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official
"participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her
position and without significant substantive or intervening review, the official negotiates,


File No. I-07-188
Page No.4
advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental
decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision if, for the purpose of influencing the
decision, the official contacts or appears before or otherwise attempts to influence, any
member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.3.)
Councilmember Schaffner is making, participating in making, or influencing a
governmental decision when acting in her role as a member of the Healdsburg City
Council.2
Step 3. What are Councilmember Schaffner's economic interests?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising
from certain enumerated economic interests. These economic interests are described in
Section 87103 and Regulations 18703-18703.5, inclusive:
o A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he
or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a);
Regulation 18703. 1(a).)
o A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or
she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.
(Section 87 103(d); Regulation 18703. l(b).)
o A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she
has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation
r8703.2.)
o An official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, totaling $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
87 103(c); Regulation 18703.3.)
o A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her
if the gifts total $390 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e);
Regulation 18703.4.)
2 When a public official who holds an office specified in section 87200 has a conflict of interest in
a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: ( l) immediately prior to the discussion of the
item, orally identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the
economic interest, as discussed in regulation 18702.5(b), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or
herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed
sessions, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member ofthe public regarding personal interests,
special rules found in regulation 18702.5(c) and 18702.5(d) apply.


File No. I-07-188
Page No. 5
o A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. This is
commonly referred to as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103;
Regulation 18703.5.)
Your facts implicate the SCA and possibly several of its members as sources of
income to Councilmember Schaffner.
SCA
Because Councilmember Schaffner receives a salary from the SCA to serve as its
executive director (presumed to be at least $500 per year), the SCA is a source of income
to her.
Members of SCA
Tony Korman Development, Saggio Hill Development Company and Tony
Korman may be sources of income to Councilmember Schaffner through the SCA.
However, the general rule is that members of a nonprofit organization such as the SCA
are not considered to be sources of income to an official unless one, or a few, of the
nonprofit organization's members actually control the organization's decisions. (Lucas
Advice Letter, No. 4-96-248.) You state that neither Tony Korman Development nor
Saggio Hill Development Company, either acting alone or in concert with other members
of the SCA, controls the SCA's decisions. You also indicate that the terms of
Councilmember Schaffner's employment are supervised entirely by the three members of
the Executive Committee who serye as the SCA's executive officers. While Mr. Korman
serves on the eighteen-member executive committee, the committee itself does not
participate in, nor take any action regarding, the contract pursuant to which
Councilmember Schaffner is employed. Under these facts, it is not appropriate to pierce
through the nonprofit organization's corporate structure to any of its members. The
individual members of the SCA, including Tony Korman Development, Saggio Hill
Development Company, and their principal, Tony Korman, will not be economic interests
of Councilmember Schaffner by virtue of membership in the SCA.
Step 4. Will Councilmember Schaffner's economic interests be directly or
indirectly involved in decisions she will make, participate in making or influence as
a public oflicial?
A person, including business entities and sources of income, is directly involved
in a decision before an official's agency when that person, either directly or by an agent
(1) initiates the proceeding by filing an application, claim, appeal or similar request; or
(2) is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding. (Regulation 18704.1.)
Because the SCA will not be initiating any proceeding regarding the Saggio Hills
development application before the city council nor will it be a named party or the


File No. I-07-188
Page No. 6
subject of the proceeding, the SCA is not directly involved. Therefore, it is indirectly
involved. (See Regulation 18704. 1(b).)
Step 5. What is the applicable materiality standard?
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial
effect of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material.
(Regulation 1 8700(a).)
Regulation 18705.3(b)(2)(A)-(F) sets forth different materiality standards for
sources of income that are nonprofit entities, depending on the size of the entity.
According to your facts, the annual gross receipts of SCA are between $100,000 and
$1,000,000, so the following standards apply:
"(2) Sources of income which are non-profit entities,
including governmental entities. The effect of a decision is
material as to a nonprofit entity which is a source of
income to the official if any of the following applies: . . .
"(E) For an entity whose gross annual receipts are more
than $100,000 but less than or equal to $1,000,000 the
effect of the decision will be any of the following:
"(i) The decision will result in an increase or decrease of
the entity's gross annual receipts for a fiscal year in the
amount of $50,000 or more.
"(ii) The decision will cause the entity to incur or avoid
additional expenses or to reduce or eliminate existing
expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $12,500 or
more.
"(iii) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the value of the entity's assets or liabilities in the amount of
$50,000. (Regulation 18705.3(b)(2XE).)"
Nexus Test
In addition to the materiality standard described above, there is also a separate
materiality standard that applies in cases where there is a "nexus" between duties owed to
a source of income and to the official's public agency. The materiality threshold is
understandably much lower when a public official is paid by a private entity to
accomplish some action that is within the official's public decisionmaking authority. The
"nexus test" is set out at Regulation 18705.3(c):


File No. I-07-188
Page No. 7
"(c) Nexus . Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on
a person who is a source of income to a public official is
deemed material if the public official receives or is
promised the income to achieve a goal or purpose which
would be achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by the
decision." (Emphasis added.)
The rationale for the nexus test is that when an employee eams a salary to
accomplish a purpose that may be advanced by what he or she does as a public official,
we presume that the employer is benefiting from the actions of the employee in his or her
official capacity. (Yarnell Advice Letter, No. A-00-161.) Typically, a "nexus,'is found
in situations where the official is also a high-level employee with direct influence and
control over his or her employer's management or policy decisions. (Moser Advice
Letter, No. A-03-147; Low Advice Letter, No. 4-99-305.)
Here, Councilmember Schaffner is the executive director of the SCA, a position
in which she develops written policy statements on matters relating to the business
environment within Sonoma County. It follows that she has influence over the
nonprofit's policy decisions. She receives a salary from the SCA and attempts to further
the organization's goals of promoting the business climate and economic well-being of
Sonoma County. While you do not indicate whether the SCA has taken a position
regarding the Saggio Hills Project, it can be presumed that a development project of this
nature would further the enunciated goals of the SCA. In the instance of decisions
approving the Saggio Hills Project, it appears that the SCA's goals overlap with
Councilmember Schaffner's decisionmaking authority as a city councilmember. Because
she receives income to achieve a goal or purpose that would be aided by the city council
decisions, the nexus test applies.
Step 6. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the applicable materiality standards
will be met?
Under the nexus test, we must examine whether the city council's decisions on the
Saggio Hills Project will have "any reasonably foreseeable financial effect" on the SCA.
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there is a
substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect need
not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (ln re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.)
You have not provided facts from which we can determine whether city council
decisions to approve the Saggio Hills Project will have a financial effect on the SCA. If,
for example, a decision to approve some portion of the project is substantially likely to
cause an increase in the membership of the SCA, this would have a financial effect on the


File No. I-07-188
Page No. 8
organization. This is a fact-dependent decision that will require Councilmember
Schaffner to consider the circumstances surrounding a specific decision in order to
determine if the nexus test applies.
Steps 7. and 8. The "public generally" and "legally required participation,,
exceptions.
Even if a material financial effect on a public official's economic interest is
reasonably foreseeable, he or she still may not be disqualified if the financial effect of the
governmental decision on the public official's economic interest is indistinguishable from
its effect on the public generally (Section 87103, Regulations 18700(b)(7) and 18707(a)),
or if the official is legally required to participate (Section 87103; Regulation 18703).
You have not presented any facts indicating that either of these exceptions is applicable
to your situation.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, I*gal Division
VJjgl

including governmental entities. The effect of a decision is
material as to a nonprofit entity which is a source of
income to the official if any of the following applies: . . .

than $100,000 but less than or equal to $1,000,000 the
effect of the decision will be any of the following:

the entity's gross annual receipts for a fiscal year in the

regarding the Saggio Hills Project, it can be presumed that a development project of this
nature would further the enunciated goals of the SCA. In the instance of decisions
approving the Saggio Hills Project, it appears that the SCA's goals overlap with
Councilmember Schaffner's decisionmaking authority as a city councilmember. Because
she receives income to achieve a goal or purpose that would be aided by the city council
decisions, the nexus test applies.
Step 6. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the applicable materiality standards
will be met?
Under the nexus test, we must examine whether the city council's decisions on the
Saggio Hills Project will have "any reasonably foreseeable financial effect" on the SCA.
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there is a
substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect need
not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (ln re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.)
You have not provided facts from which we can determine whether city council
decisions to approve the Saggio Hills Project will have a financial effect on the SCA. If,
for example, a decision to approve some portion of the project is substantially likely to
cause an increase in the membership of the SCA, this would have a financial effect on the
organization. This is a fact-dependent decision that will require Councilmember
Schaffner to consider the circumstances surrounding a specific decision in order to
determine if the nexus test applies.
Steps 7. and 8. The "public generally" and "legally required participation,,
exceptions.
Even if a material financial effect on a public official's economic interest is
reasonably foreseeable, he or she still may not be disqualified if the financial effect of the
governmental decision on the public official's economic interest is indistinguishable from
its effect on the public generally (Section 87103, Regulations 18700(b)(7) and 18707(a)),
or if the official is legally required to participate (Section 87103; Regulation 18703).
You have not presented any facts indicating that either of these exceptions is applicable
to your situation.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: l. Do the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions bar Councilmember Schaffner
from participating in governmental decisions that will have a financial effect on business
entities that are members of the Sonoma County Alliance, a nonprofit entity which
Councilmember Schaffner serves as its executive director?
2. Does the "nexus test" disqualify Councilmember Schaffner from making,
participating in making, or influencing city council decisions that may affect the Sonoma
County Alliance?
rfl2r

CONCLUSION: 1. No. Councilmember Schaffner does not have an economic interest in the
business entities merely by virtue of their membership in the Sonoma County Alliance
provided, however, that either acting alone or in concert with other members, they do not
control the decisions of the Sonoma County Alliance.
2. If a city council decision regarding the Saggio Hills Project will have any
reasonably foreseeable financial effect on the Sonoma County Alliance, then the nexus
test would operate to disqualify Councilmember Schaffner from making, participating in
making, or influencing that decision.

Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(cX3).) Please also note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. QUESTTONS l. Do the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions bar Councilmember Schaffner from participating in governmental decisions that will have a financial effect on business entities that are members of the Sonoma County Alliance, a nonprofit entity which Councilmember Schaffner serves as its executive director? 2. Does the "nexus test" disqualify Councilmember Schaffner from making, participating in making, or influencing city council decisions that may affect the Sonoma County Alliance? ' The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory ref'erences are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulationi of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through I tt997 of Title 2 of the Calitbrnia Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the Califbrnia Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. rfl2r File No. I-07-188 Page No. 2 CONCLUSIONS 1. No. Councilmember Schaffner does not have an economic interest in the business entities merely by virtue of their membership in the Sonoma County Alliance provided, however, that either acting alone or in concert with other members, they do not control the decisions of the Sonoma County Alliance. 2. If a city council decision regarding the Saggio Hills Project will have any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on the Sonoma County Alliance, then the nexus test would operate to disqualify Councilmember Schaffner from making, participating in making, or influencing that decision. FACTS The Sonoma County Alliance (the "SCA") is a voluntary membership organization composed of business, agricultural, labor, and individual interests focused on education and advocacy concerning business and economic issues. It is organized as a California nonprofit corporation under Section 501(cX7) of the Intemal Revenue Code. The purpose of the SCA is to promote the business climate and economic wellbeing of Sonoma County. In this regard, the SCA seeks to provide input to elected officials on matters involving public policy, economic development, housing, taxation, and similar matters. The SCA also encourages its members
